title: Inventory of the Estate – How a Court Bailiff Compiles an Inventory in Poland
description: Learn when an estate inventory is required in Poland, how the bailiff compiles it, and what its legal consequences are for heirs and creditors.
content:
What Is an Estate Inventory?

An estate inventory (spis inwentarza) is a formal official document compiled by a court bailiff, containing a list of the assets and liabilities forming part of an estate or a debtor's property. Its purpose is to establish the precise state of the estate for the needs of court proceedings – most commonly succession or enforcement proceedings.

Legal Basis

The compilation of an estate inventory is governed by:

  • The Civil Code (Arts. 1031–1040) – regarding the heir's liability for estate debts and the consequences of the inventory,
  • The Code of Civil Procedure (Arts. 637–643) – the procedure for compiling the inventory,
  • The Act on Court Bailiffs of 22 March 2018.

When Is an Estate Inventory Required?

An estate inventory is compiled most commonly in the following situations:

  • Acceptance of the estate with benefit of inventory – the heir's liability for estate debts is then limited to the value of the assets listed in the inventory,
  • At the request of a creditor of the deceased – to establish the assets forming part of the estate,
  • At the request of an heir or legatee – to determine the scope of the inherited estate,
  • In enforcement proceedings – when the bailiff is required to compile an inventory of the debtor's property.

The Inventory Procedure

The bailiff acts on the basis of a court order (or a notarial certificate) and visits the location where the assets are situated – usually the deceased's last place of residence or the debtor's premises. The bailiff draws up a detailed description and valuation of all movable assets, real property, receivables, bank balances and liabilities. The resulting protocol is submitted to the court.

Legal Consequences

The inventory is an official document with evidential weight in court proceedings. Where an estate is accepted with benefit of inventory, it sets the ceiling on the heir's liability for debts. Any inaccuracies in the asset list may give rise to liability for damages.

Inventory List vs. Estate Inventory

Note the distinction between the court bailiff's inventory (compiled by the bailiff on the basis of a court order) and a self-compiled inventory list (drawn up independently by the heir and filed with the court or notary). The bailiff's version carries greater evidential weight.

Costs

The costs of the inventory procedure are initially borne by the applicant as an advance and are ultimately allocated between the parties in accordance with the outcome of the proceedings.
